column2: How do we deal with missing values in a variable that we want to analyse
column3: We fill in a reasonable value
column4: We can ignore them in the analysis, but need to report them
column5: We make them a separate level of the variable

column2: Glasgow coma score is used to objectively describe the extent of impaired consciousness. The possible values are 15, 14, 13,..., 4 and 3. This variable is of type
In a boxplot, we can immediately read off
column4: The 25 and 75 percentile and the median
column6: The mean and the standard deviation

column2: We make a scatterplot to visualize the relation between two numeric variables. Both have a skewed distribution. We want to quantify the strength of the association. What is the best way to do this?
column5: Both Spearman and Pearson correlation can be used
column6: Neither Spearman nor Pearson correlation can be used. We use the Wilcoxon signed-rank test

column2: We use linear regression to study the relation between a numeric outcome variable and a numeric independent variable. Both have a skewed distribution. Do we need to transform both?
column3: Yes, variables with a skewed distribution always need to be transformed
column4: The outcome variable needs to be transformed in order to make the residuals normal. There is no need to transform the independent variable. It only makes it harder to interpret
column5: The outcome variable needs to be transformed in order to make the residuals normal. It is useful to transform the independent variable if it makes the relation with the outcome more linear
column6: There is no need to transform. Transforming is only used to make mean and median more similar

column2: We want to test whether the white blood cell count (wbc) in the cerebrospinal fluid differs between patients with tuberculous meningitis (TBM) and cryptococcal meningitis (CM). How do we formulate the null hypothesis?
column3: There is no significant difference in mean wbc between TBM and CM patients
column4: The mean wbc differs between TBM and CM patients
column5: The 95% confidence interval for the difference in mean wbc between TBM and CM patients does not cover the value 0
column6: There is no difference in mean wbc between TBM and CM patients

column2: In a large randomized controlled trial we study whether dexamethasone reduces mortality in patients with tuberculous meningitis. The estimate of the odds ratio of the risk of dying within one year was 0.86, with 95% confidence interval 0.52-1.41 and a p-value of 0.55. What do we conclude with respect to the null hypothesis OR=1?
column4: The probability that the null hypothesis is correct is 55%
column5: If the null hypothesis is correct, then the probability to obtain an estimate of OR<0.86 or OR>1/0.86=1.16 is 55%
column6: The probability to reject the null hypothesis, when in fact it is correct, is 45%

column2: What does it mean that the 95% confidence interval (CI) for the odds ratio is 0.52-1.41?
column3: With 95% probability the true OR is between 0.52 and 1.41
column4: If we repeat the experiment many times under exactly the same conditions, then the OR estimate will be between 0.52 and 1.41 in 95% of these experiments
column5: If we repeat the experiment many times under exactly the same conditions, then in 95% of these experiments the 95% CI will cover the true value
column6: None of the three is correct

column2: What is the difference between standard deviation and standard error?
column3: The standard error is the name given to the standard deviation of the estimator of a population parameter
column4: The standard deviation quantifies the spread in the measured values of a variable in the sample, while the standard error quantifies the spread in the values in the population that the sample represents
column5: Standard error and standard deviation are two names of the same quantity
column6: The standard deviation quantifies the spread in the measured values of a variable in the sample, while the standard error is the probability of falsely rejecting the null hypothesis

column2: A researcher wants to compare the mean cholesterol levels between a group of smokers and a group of non-smokers. The data in both groups are normally distributed. Which statistical test is the most appropriate to use?

column3: Comparing the weight of 50 men vs. 50 women
column4: Comparing the recovery time of patients using Drug A vs. patients using Drug B
column5: Comparing the systolic blood pressure of the same group of patients before and after taking a new medication
column6: Comparing the test scores of students in two different cities

column2: You are comparing the length of hospital stays (in days) between two groups of patients. You notice that the sample size is small (n = 12 per group) and the data is highly skewed with several outliers. Which test should you use to compare these two groups?

A study compares the effect of two different diets on weight loss. After performing a t-test, the researchers report a p-value of 0.02. Using a significance level ($\alpha$) of 0.05, what is the most appropriate conclusion?
column3: Fail to reject the null hypothesis; there is no evidence of a difference
column4: Reject the null hypothesis; there is a statistically significant difference between the diets

In a simple linear regression model where Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP) is the dependent variable and Age is the independent variable, the resulting equation is SBP = 105 + 0.45*Age. What is the correct interpretation of the slope (0.45)?
column3: The average SBP for all patients is 105 mmHg

column3: The independent variable must follow a normal distribution
column4: The relationship between x and y must be roughly linear

A logistic regression analysis finds that the Odds Ratio for mortality in patients with secondary dengue infection compared to primary infection is 3.2 (95% CI: 1.8 Đ 5.4). What is the correct interpretation of this result?
column3: Patients with secondary infection are 3.2 times more likely to survive
column4: The probability of death is 3.2% higher in the secondary infection group
column5: The odds of death are 3.2 times higher in patients with secondary infection compared to primary infection
